text: The laparoscopic view reveals a small bowel obstruction caused by a tight adhesion between the sigmoid colon and the lateral pelvic wall. The surgeon identifies a necrotic loop of ileum, indicating strangulation.
The surgeon uses an energy device to perform lysis of adhesion, releasing the tight adhesion between the sigmoid colon and the lateral pelvic wall.

Following the lysis of adhesion, the surgeon brings the strangulated loop of necrotic ileum into the abdominal cavity. The abdomen is irrigated with antibiotic solution, and a small bowel resection is performed using an endo-GIA stapler.
The surgeon uses an energy device to ligate and divide the mesentery of the small bowel, separating it from the surrounding tissue.

Following the resection of the small bowel using the endo-GIA stapler, the resected specimen is left in the pelvis. The surgeon then performs a primary anastomosis between the loops of ileum using intracorporeal suturing technique.

A stay stitch is placed and secured to the abdominal wall to facilitate intracorporeal anastomosis. Two enterotomies are created on the proximal and distal portions of the small bowel, and a staple is inserted into both limbs to perform the primary anastomosis using the endo-GIA device.

The surgeon inspects the anastomosis site, confirming the absence of bleeding. The opening of the anastomosis is then closed using a GIA stapler and reinforced with 2-0 Ethibond sutures in a simple, interrupted pattern.
The surgeon closes the mesentery defect using Tuo Etaban sutures in a simple running fashion, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
